Curriculum → Case studies and interview drills → Case study: Twitter timeline and search

Lesson 8 of 9

Case study: Twitter timeline and search

Design tweet ingestion, fan-out, home timelines, user timelines, and search while dealing with huge read volume, celebrity skew, and asynchronous notifications.

35-45 min Case studies and interview drills 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Twitter is a classic interview problem because it forces you to explain fan-out trade-offs, caching, storage separation, and search indexing under extreme scale.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Tweet write path and fan-out Keep tweet creation fast with durable writes plus asynchronous fan-out2. Timeline reads Cache recent home-timeline entries for active users3. Search and operational skew Index tweets asynchronously in a search cluster

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Twitter is a classic interview problem because it forces you to explain fan-out trade-offs, caching, storage separation, and search indexing under extreme scale. 2. Tweet write path and fan-out Keep tweet creation fast with durable writes plus asynchronous fan-out3. Timeline reads Cache recent home-timeline entries for active users4. Search and operational skew Index tweets asynchronously in a search cluster
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Twitter is a classic interview problem because it forces you to explain fan-out trade-offs, caching, storage separation, and search indexing under extreme scale.

What to say first

  • Separate tweet creation from fan-out and search indexing.
  • Explain the difference between home and user timelines.
  • Handle celebrity skew explicitly.
2

Study section

Tweet write path and fan-out

Posting a tweet should feel fast, even though the downstream work is much larger. The write path should persist the tweet, enqueue fan-out, and decouple notifications from storage.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Keep tweet creation fast with durable writes plus asynchronous fan-out
  • Use different strategies for normal users and celebrity accounts
  • Store media separately from tweet metadata
3

Study section

Timeline reads

Home timelines and user timelines have different access patterns. Home feeds often rely on cached or precomputed lists, while user timelines can read more directly from tweet storage.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Cache recent home-timeline entries for active users
  • Limit timeline length in memory and rebuild cold feeds lazily
  • Use multiget-style reads for tweet and user metadata
4

Study section

Search and operational skew

Search indexing, notifications, and celebrity fan-out all create side effects that can dominate the architecture if they are not isolated well.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Index tweets asynchronously in a search cluster
  • Handle celebrity tweets with pull or hybrid fan-out strategies
  • Track fan-out lag, cache hit rate, and index freshness

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Separate tweet creation from fan-out and search indexing.
  • Explain the difference between home and user timelines.
  • Handle celebrity skew explicitly.
  • Mention how search stays fresh without blocking writes.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Trying to fan out synchronously to every follower on the write path.
  • Using one storage model for tweets, timelines, media, and search.
  • Ignoring cold-start rebuilds and cache eviction behavior.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When would you push timelines versus pull them at read time?
  • How does a celebrity account change the fan-out design?
  • What part of the system would you scale first as reads explode?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Queues, streams, and background work Caching, asynchrony, and resilienceSearch and autocomplete Common product design patternsCaching layers and cache placement Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Twitter timeline and search

Take the product shape from the case study and test one read path plus one write or ingestion path under pressure. Apply it to case study: twitter timeline and search so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Twitter timeline and search" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around tweet write path and fan-out and watch how it interacts with timeline reads.Twitter is a classic interview problem because it forces you to explain fan-out trade-offs, caching, storage separation, and search indexing under extreme scale.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • read p95
  • cache hit ratio
  • derived store utilization
  • queue backlog

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

7 nodes

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

topology.flow Edit the topology and override script with quick inserts, inline diagnostics, and visible structure signals.

Current file summary

7 nodes · 6 links

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 90000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 26000 rps · 12 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 110000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 4: database · 8500 rps · 20 ms
  • Line 5: database · 18000 rps · 18 ms
  • Line 6: queue · 32000 rps · 10 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Separate tweet creation from fan-out and search indexing.
  • Explain the difference between home and user timelines.
  • Handle celebrity skew explicitly.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Tweet write path and fan-out and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Separate tweet creation from fan-out and search indexing. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Timeline reads.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

design

Interview opening

Not saved yet

You are starting a mock interview for "Case study: Twitter timeline and search". Capture the product assumptions, scale estimates, core APIs, and the first diagram you would draw.

Goal for this step

Frame the problem, scope, assumptions, and success metrics before you draw components.

Use these guardrails

  • When would you push timelines versus pull them at read time?
  • Tweet write path and fan-out
  • Timeline reads
  • Separate tweet creation from fan-out and search indexing.

Suggested structure

  • Requirements and assumptions
  • Scale estimate
  • Core APIs
  • First diagram

Bring your own model
